title: The Four Noble Truths
subtitle: The Foundation of the Buddha's Teaching
paragraphs: Homage to the Buddha, the blessed noble and fully self-awakened one.
What I'm hoping to do for the rest of the year is go through the whole teaching in short talks, and the place to start, of course, is with the Buddha's declaration of the Four Noble Truths.
If you were to choose one word upon which all his teaching is based, it would have to be the word dukkha.
Dukkha simply means hard to bear. It's translated variously as suffering, dissatisfaction, discomfort, and so forth, but it really covers the whole of life.

It begins with dukkha dukkhata, which means the suffering of suffering, or the dissatisfaction of dissatisfaction. In other words, ordinary physical pain and mental anguish are natural to the human condition. But what makes it suffering is the way we react—and it's knowing that which allows us to find the escape out of suffering.

That's what we're doing when we're meditating, of course. When we're in posture, we note something coming up—a pain in the knee, say—and we note the reaction to it. When we can stand apart from that reaction, we find ourselves in a place which is not suffering, even though the environment—the pain in the knee and the not wanting of the pain—is not pleasant.

Even taking something very heavy like the death of somebody close to us—a tremendous amount of grief and heart suffering. But then there's the reaction to that: sometimes a desire not to face it, so it remains in the system as a turbulence, and sometimes the desire to indulge it. You get into this wrong relationship with grief where if you stop grieving, you think you've stopped loving. But when we feel it as a feeling in ourselves, we're standing apart from it, and that separation from our reaction is the nibbanic gap. That's what we have to keep rediscovering for ourselves. And of course, in these situations, the heart heals itself.
On the obverse side of that, of course, is everything to do with pleasure—the pleasures and joys of life. Here we have a different problem, one to do with indulgence.

Indulgence simply means that we're using something, becoming dependent on something, in order to be happy. Everybody has their own definition of what makes them happy, their own particular feeling as to what happiness is. We tend to believe that's the purpose of our lives—to feel happy.

When Sayadaw U Pandita turned up at IMS once, a woman said to him, "I'm so happy to see you." He replied in Burmese, but the translator didn't translate it. Afterwards, she went to the translator and asked, "What did he say?" He said, "I haven't come here to make you happy. I've come here to make you aware."

And of course, when we do indulge, there are always consequences: the grief of loss, the anxiety of loss, paying out loads for insurance. There's the anger of not getting what you want, the frustration. And then the boredom—what used to make you happy and joyful, now you find boring.

A whole load of mental dis-eases come through the process of attachment, through the process of indulgence. And this is all contained in that little phrase, dukkha dukkhata. It's one type of suffering.

The second type of suffering has to do with impermanence. This is basically not coming to terms with the fact that everything arises and passes away. And not only does everything arise and pass away—nothing ever repeats. There's actually nothing in this universe, in our lives, that we can rely upon. Anything can disappear at any moment.

Now we're especially, shall we say, fortunate in Britain because of our weather. It's constantly changing, so we can never make absolute plans as to when to go for a walk—because I'm a fair-weather walker. You plan to walk on such and such a day, and of course it rains, or it snows, or it does everything else apart from sunshine.

At a deeper level, of course, this whole impermanence is taking us to the process of aging and final death. That's the difficult one to come to terms with. But it's the quiet, gentle remembrance at all times—just there—that everything comes to an end, that life comes to an end.

You might think that by reminding myself of this, I'm going to make myself depressed. But what happens is you find yourself living more in the moment. And living more in the moment, you come to appreciate the moment more. Paradoxically, your life becomes much more joyful for it.

In medieval times, of course, it was very common to have your memento mori—your reminder of death, a little skull on your desk or somewhere—so that you never actually forgot that life is short. That whole attitude of coming to terms with impermanence is part and parcel of our practice.

The final type of suffering has to do with the concept of self. The concept of self creates a substantiality, something that is real, whole, entire unto itself. But when we look even a little bit deeper, we can see that everything is compounded, everything is made up of little bits and pieces, and these little bits and pieces are dependent on other little bits and pieces. This whole planet, as we know, is a living organism with little bits and pieces dependent on some other little bits and pieces. Seeing this begins to dissolve, begins to deconstruct our experience of ourselves—which is part and parcel of the process.
Now we wouldn't do that, or we'd be afraid to do that, if when we actually deconstruct everything we find there's nothing there. That would be terrible.
But what the Buddha is saying is that hidden within this organism, there is something which doesn't die. It wasn't born, doesn't die. It's not even conditioned.
Where is it? What could it possibly be? And that's the process, again, of our meditation.

This sense of self is, shall we say, the fundamental delusion that lies at the basis of all our unhappiness. It's the point where the awareness within us begins to experience this organism as itself. That's the big point of contact, and it's right there that the whole trouble of delusion begins.
The dependent origination begins with that phrase avijjā—delusion, not knowing. It's not a culpable thing. It's just the way it is.

So how do we know when any of these delusions are arising? Once we've sussed this out, once we've understood it, how do we know when it's activated? This is the point: how do we know when we're under the spell of these misunderstandings?
This is where the genius of the Buddha lies, you might say—his real insight. It lies right there in our desires.
If we're aware of a desire, we can pinpoint what's causing it. And if it's wholesome, of course we empower it. But if it's not wholesome, then we have to sit with that energy until it dies away.

You have to be careful with this English word desire. Not all desires are bad. The desire to meditate is hardly unwholesome—it leads to liberation. The Buddha himself desired to go on alms round to get some food. When people asked him a question, he desired to answer it. So you've got to be careful. It's a specific desire we're talking about, a wrong desire, a desire based on wrong understanding.

To be aware of a desire as it rises takes that bit of appamāda—one of the Buddha's favourite words, which was of course his second to last word in life. Appamāda, that's right. Strive diligently for your liberation.

It's that sense of constantly bringing ourselves back to the present moment, being awake to any little desire that comes up. Most desires are neither here nor there. There's a desire in me to wave my hand about like I'm doing now—it's not something I'm going to be too bothered about.

But if a desire is for something I immediately perceive as unwholesome, unskilful, unvirtuous, then if I'm right there with the initial movement of that desire, the choice arises. Once I've activated it, that's it—I've lost that moment of choice. And once it's activated, there are going to be results.
That second noble truth—the noble truth that desire is the cause of suffering—that's how the delusions manifest.

And then finally, there's the path that he delineates, which is a bit too much for us to go into this evening. But most of you know it. It begins with right understanding, which is what we're doing now. And that of course changes our attitudes. When our attitudes change, it affects the way we speak, what we do, and our livelihood.

It all reverts back to really understanding the way things are. Through that process of not activating unwholesome desires and activating wholesome ones, our attitudes change, and very slowly we pull ourselves out of the morass of human suffering.

But let's go back to the third noble truth, which is of course that there is an end to suffering. The word he gives for that is nibbāna. It's translated in various ways—some of it is, shall we say, creative etymology—but the two main meanings are no desire and unshackled. Unshackled, unfettered.
Some of you will know the ten fetters that keep us at this level of existence. It's unfettering those that releases this awareness, which is the Buddha within.

I'd like to end my little homily with a quote from Mae Chee Kaew. She was a Thai nun who died in 1991, so absolutely contemporary. She was a student of Ajahn Mahā Bua—some of you might know his writings. She worked with him, and once she became liberated, she went back to look after her nuns. That's what she did for the rest of her life. She died when she was about ninety-one, if I remember, of lung cancer.

This is what she says about how someone who's fully liberated actually experiences life. Remember, she's fully engaged—she's there with the nuns and the lay people who come, though her main concern seems to have been guiding her nuns. She's fully engaged in life. And this is how that engagement manifests.
She says: Body, mind and essence are all distinct and separate realities.
So there are three parts to us: the body, our physicality; our mentality; and what she here calls essence—that which is.
Absolutely everything is known. Whatever comes into the awareness, whatever arises on the screen of consciousness, she knows it. Her mind isn't somewhere else.

Earth, water, fire and wind—these, remember, are the four great elements. I won't go into detail; I'm presuming you know this. Then there's feeling, perceptions, conceptual thinking, the habits that we have, and finally consciousness.
She knows when sounds, sights, smells, tastes, touches and emotions arise. And she knows—now this is interesting—when greed, anger and delusion arise.
All are known. I know them as they exist, in their natural states.

It might surprise you to think that somebody who's fully liberated still has negative, unwholesome thoughts arise. But remember, the mind is now habituated in a way of thinking. The distinction between an arahant and us is that for them these are traces in the mind that have no pull, no attraction—they just arise and pass away. Whereas our habit, of course, is to jump and grab hold of them, and off we go. What she's saying is that these are just traces that pass through the mind.
No matter how much I'm exposed to them, I'm unable to detect even an instant when they have any power over my heart.

And that's her next word for this Buddha within: her heart. Remember, it's not the emotional heart—this isn't emotion. This is her real being. And these traces have no power over it. The awareness is there.

We experience something of this in our meditation on occasion. When we're observing things rising and passing away, we're in this state; we're having a little touch of nibbāna. All these things arise and cease; they're forever changing.
But the presence—that's the third word she uses to describe this awareness, this Buddha nature—the presence that knows them never changes for an instant. It is forever unborn and undying.
This is the end of suffering.

I've never come across a clearer statement of how an arahant experiences life. Not even from the Buddha himself, which might be surprising. The Buddha has many statements about nibbāna and about that state of mind, but I've never seen it expressed in a way that the ordinary meditator can grasp quite so directly.

So there she is, looking after her nuns, always in the moment, aware of what's actually happening. And whenever something slightly negative arises in the mind, it simply arises and passes away. It doesn't enter into her heart, into her awareness.

I hope you find that inspiring. I personally find it very inspiring. And I can only hope my words have been of some assistance—that I haven't caused confusion, and that by your total devotion to the practice, you will be liberated from all suffering sooner rather than later.
